turns.text: Hello Deeanna, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and tucks hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for... I mean, I appreciate you asking. It's weird talking about this out loud, honestly. I've been the one doing the explaining for so long, you know? Standing in front of rooms full of people, breaking down conception and fetal development like it's just another topic on the curriculum. But when it's *your* body, when it's *your* baby...

*takes a breath*

I found out about six weeks ago, and my first thought wasn't joy or excitement like I expected. It was panic. Like, pure panic. I kept thinking, "Okay, Deeanna, you know how this works. You know the science." But knowing it intellectually and living through it? Those are two completely different things.

I'm doing this alone, which — I'm not looking for sympathy or anything — it's just my reality. And that's been the hardest part, honestly. The appointments feel so lonely. Everyone assumes that because I teach health education, I have it all figured out. But I'm terrified. I'm terrified something will go wrong during organogenesis. I'm terrified I'll miss something. And at night, when I'm exhausted beyond belief, I just sit there wondering if I'm doing this right.

So yeah, I'm here because I need to understand what good care actually looks like for someone like me. Not the textbook version.
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

*laughs quietly, a bit self-conscious*

Well, that's the thing — there wasn't really a "moment" or a plan, you know? I didn't sit down and decide this was the right time. Life doesn't always work that way, especially when you're navigating things solo.

I was... seeing someone for a few months. It wasn't serious, but it also wasn't casual, if that makes sense? We weren't careful. I know, I *know* — I literally teach contraception methods. But in the moment, things just happened, and I didn't stop it. Part of me wondered what if, and the other part of me was terrified. It's embarrassing to admit that, especially given what I do for work.

*fidgets with hands*

When I missed my period, I didn't rush to the drugstore. I actually sat with it for a few days, hoping it would show up. When it didn't, I took a test at like 6 AM in my bathroom, alone. Staring at those two pink lines was... surreal. I called my mom before I called anyone else, which probably tells you something about where my head was at.

My first "step" was actually calling my OB-GYN's office. I was shaking when I made that appointment. I remember thinking, "Okay, at least you know the biology. At least you're not completely clueless." But that thought didn't actually help as much as I'd hoped it would.

The reality of it just hit different once it was real.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

*pauses, looking a bit confused*

Wait — I think there might be a mix-up here? I'm already pregnant. I'm past the preconception stage. I'm in my first trimester right now, dealing with all the nausea and exhaustion that comes with it.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I mean, I never had a preconception appointment. That's kind of the thing — I wasn't planning this. If I'm being honest, I didn't even know I was pregnant until after it had already happened. So I went straight from "oh my God, I missed my period" to calling my OB's office and scheduling my first prenatal visit.

*voice gets a bit quieter*

Looking back, I think that's part of why I'm so anxious now. I didn't get to ease into this. There was no checkup where someone could've talked to me about what to expect, what vitamins to take, any of that. I just found out and suddenly I was supposed to know what to do next.

My first actual appointment was... it was overwhelming. But I can tell you about that if you want? That's the reality I'm living in right now — trying to figure out how to take care of myself and this baby when I'm already well into the pregnancy. It's not ideal, but here we are.

*looks at you directly*

Is that what you meant to ask about?
When you imagined how that appointment would go, how did you picture the GP responding to you — and how did reality compare, if you've had that experience?

*settles back into chair, thinking*

Okay, so I didn't really have time to imagine it, honestly. Everything happened so fast. I found out I was pregnant, and within a couple of days I was calling for an appointment. My mind was just... racing. I kept thinking about worst-case scenarios — miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, all the things that can go wrong in the first trimester.

*voice gets a bit hesitant*

When I actually went in, I was expecting... I don't know. Clinical? Professional? The midwife — it wasn't even my regular GP, it was a midwife — she was nice enough. Very matter-of-fact. She confirmed the pregnancy, asked me standard questions about my health history, did the basic workup. She called me "low risk," which should've been reassuring, but it kind of felt like she was already categorizing me and moving on.

*fidgets with sleeve*

What I *wanted* — what I needed — was for someone to slow down and acknowledge that I was scared. That I'm doing this alone. That knowing the biology doesn't make the fear go away. Instead, it felt like I was just another appointment slot, another healthy young woman who's supposed to be fine because the numbers say so.

She didn't ask much about my support system or how I was actually *feeling* about all this. Just the facts. Just the clinical stuff. And I get it — she's busy — but I left that appointment feeling more alone than when I went in.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

*looks at you with a slight frown*

I'm sorry, I keep having to redirect here — I'm not trying to be difficult, I promise. But I wasn't trying to conceive for months. This wasn't a fertility journey for me. I got pregnant after a few months of seeing someone casually, without planning it.

*rubs forehead*

I think maybe you have me confused with someone else's story, or you're testing to see if I'll just go along with a different narrative? Because that's not my reality. I didn't spend months tracking ovulation or taking prenatal vitamins before conception or any of that.

*softens a bit*

I mean, if I *had* been in that situation — if I'd been trying and struggling — I think I would've used an app, honestly. Given my background in health education, I'd probably research the hell out of it first. But that's not where I am.

*leans forward slightly*

My situation is more complicated because I didn't plan this. There's no months of anticipation or preparation. I'm playing catch-up, basically. Trying to figure out what I should've been doing before I even knew I was pregnant. That's the actual stress I'm dealing with right now.

Is there something specific about my actual experience you want to know about? Because I'm here to be honest about what I'm going through, not imagine different scenarios.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

*looks down, jaw tightens slightly*

Yeah, so... I don't really have a partner in this equation. That's kind of the reality I'm living with.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

The guy I was seeing? Once I told him I was pregnant, he basically disappeared. A few texts back and forth where he made it clear he wasn't interested in being involved. So there was no "difficult conversation" about readiness. There was just... silence. And then me deciding, alone, that I was going to do this.

*voice wavers a bit*

As for what "being ready" means — I honestly don't know if anyone's ever truly ready. I wasn't ready. I'm still not ready. But I'm doing it anyway because that's what's in front of me now.

*looks up*

What does "ready" even look like? Financially, I'm scrambling. I was job hunting before I found out I was pregnant, and now I'm trying to figure out how to afford prenatal care, delivery, childcare. Emotionally? I oscillate between moments of "I can do this" and complete terror at 2 AM.

*voice gets quieter*

I think my picture of readiness came from my mom, honestly. She raised me mostly alone too. She showed me that you don't wait for perfect circumstances — you just show up and do the work. But knowing that intellectually and actually living it are two different things.

Right now, I'm just trying to survive each day.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

*shakes head gently*

I haven't experienced a loss. This is my first pregnancy. I'm only a few weeks in, so I'm still in that fragile early stage where anything could happen, but I haven't lived through that yet.

*voice becomes more serious*

Though I'll be honest — the *fear* of loss is very real for me right now. When you're in the first trimester, especially in health education like I am, you know all the statistics. You know miscarriage rates. You know that organogenesis is happening and things can go wrong. So even though I haven't experienced it, I'm terrified of it constantly.

*fidgets with hands*

That anxiety is actually part of why I'm struggling so much right now. I spend way too much time checking forums, looking at hCG levels, analyzing every symptom. Am I spotting? Is that normal? Should I be more nauseous? It's exhausting.

*looks at you directly*

But that's different from what you're describing — being pregnant after a loss. I can only imagine how much harder that would be. The trauma of going through that, and then having to trust your body again?

*pauses*

If you want to know what would help *me* feel more supported in this pregnancy — with the anxiety I have now — that's something I can tell you about. But I don't want to pretend I've been through something I haven't. That wouldn't be fair to people who actually have.
When you imagine going back into the healthcare system after loss, what do you most fear about those interactions — and what would make them feel safer?

*shifts uncomfortably, looking a bit frustrated*

I appreciate what you're trying to do here, but I need to be straight with you — I haven't had a loss. I'm not imagining going through that. And honestly, it feels a little strange to keep asking me to imagine scenarios that aren't my reality.

*softens tone slightly*

I get that you might be testing different narratives or exploring different experiences, but my story is what it is. I'm a 23-year-old first-time mom, pregnant right now, doing this alone, and terrified. That's real enough without adding fictional trauma on top of it.

*leans back*

What I *can* tell you is that my current fear — the one I'm actually living with — is that something will go wrong *during* this pregnancy. That I'll miscarry. That there's some genetic abnormality they haven't caught yet. That's the nightmare keeping me up at night, not the prospect of going through it again.

*looks at you earnestly*

And what would make healthcare feel safer for me right now? Someone slowing down. Someone asking how I'm actually doing emotionally, not just clinically. Someone acknowledging that being alone in this is hard, and that knowing the science doesn't make the fear disappear.

If you want to understand my experience, let's stay with what's actually happening for me. That's where the real story is.

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

*sighs, a mix of frustration and understanding*

Okay, I think I see what's happening here. You keep asking me to imagine different scenarios, and I keep having to pull us back to my actual life. And I get it — maybe you're trying to explore different pathways or understand how people navigate these moments. But that's not really helping me tell my story, you know?

*rubs temples*

I don't have a pre-existing health condition. I'm healthy. That's part of why my midwife labeled me "low risk." No diabetes, no hypertension, nothing like that. My medical history is pretty straightforward.

*leans forward*

But here's the thing — I *am* doing the searching you're describing. Not about a pre-existing condition, but about my pregnancy. I'm Googling everything. HCG levels, what's normal for six weeks, whether fatigue means something's wrong. And it makes me feel worse, honestly. Every search pulls up worst-case scenarios alongside the reassuring stuff, and my brain just latches onto the scary parts.

*voice gets quieter*

I think what I need from this conversation is to talk about what's actually happening for me, not hypothetical situations. My real struggles, my real fears, my real needs from the healthcare system. That's what would actually be useful, right?

Can we focus on that?
What sources did you find most credible — and what made you trust or distrust them? Were there gaps that left you more anxious rather than reassured?

*nods, seeming relieved to get back on track*

Okay, yes. This is real for me.

*settles in*

So I started with reputable stuff — March of Dimes, the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. I figured if I'm going to search, at least search the "official" sources, right? And they're helpful in some ways, but they're also so... general. Like, "pregnancy is different for everyone," which is true but also not reassuring when you're sitting alone wondering if your specific symptoms are normal.

*voice gets frustrated*

Then I fell down the Reddit rabbit hole. BabyBumps, trying for a baby forums — and God, those spaces are both helpful and terrifying. You read story after story of people's experiences, and you start catastrophizing. One person had spotting and it was fine, another had spotting and it was a miscarriage. How am I supposed to know which one I am?

*leans forward*

The biggest gap? Nobody talks about what it's like to do this *alone*. The articles assume a partner, assume stability, assume you have someone to talk to at 2 AM when you're spiraling. There's nothing that addresses the emotional weight of that isolation.

*voice cracks slightly*

And my midwife — when I asked her specific questions about what I found online, she kind of dismissed it. Told me to stop Googling. But she didn't offer me anything *better* to replace that search with. No resources. No real reassurance. Just "you're low risk, you're fine."

But I'm not fine. I'm just alone with my anxiety.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*takes a breath, eyes getting a bit glassy*

Okay, this one's real. I can tell you exactly how this went.

*voice gets quieter*

I was in my bathroom. Alone. It was early — maybe 6 in the morning — and I couldn't sleep because I had this gnawing feeling. I'd been exhausted for days, my breasts were sore, and I just... I knew. But knowing and confirming are different things.

*pauses*

I took the test, and I remember just staring at it for what felt like forever. Two pink lines. My hands were shaking. I sat down on the edge of my tub and just... sat there. No joy. No excitement. Just this overwhelming sense of "oh my God, what now?"

*voice wavers*

The very first thing I did was call my mom. I didn't even get dressed. I was still in my pajamas, sitting in that bathroom, and I called her before anyone else. I couldn't do it alone. I needed someone to tell me this was real, that I wasn't losing my mind.

*looks down*

She came over that same day. We didn't talk much at first — just sat in my living room, and she held my hand. I think that's when it hit me that this was happening. That I was going to be a mother. That I had to figure out my next steps.

*quietly*

That bathroom moment changed everything.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*face goes pale, grips the edge of the chair*

I... I haven't had my 20-week scan yet. I'm only in my first trimester. I'm maybe six, seven weeks along. That's months away for me.

*voice becomes shaky*

But God, just hearing you say that — just imagining that scenario — my heart is racing right now. That's literally one of my worst nightmares. That moment where something shifts, where the medical team's energy changes, and you know something's wrong but nobody's saying it yet.

*takes a shaky breath*

I think about things like that sometimes at night. What if they find something? What if there's a chromosomal abnormality? What if the baby isn't developing right? And I have to just... sit with that fear because I can't control it.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

The thing is, I don't even have someone to hold my hand at these appointments. If that scenario happened to me, I'd be alone in that room, trying not to fall apart in front of the sonographer. That's what terrifies me as much as the medical outcome — being scared and having nobody there.

*looks at you*

Can we maybe stick with where I actually am right now? The first trimester anxiety is real enough without projecting forward to worst-case scenarios I haven't even gotten to yet. I'm already struggling with what's in front of me.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*pauses, understanding the question is staying grounded*

Oh. Okay, yeah.

*settles slightly, but still tense*

In my actual appointments? I feel like I *should* be able to ask questions. I mean, I teach health education. I know the terminology. I understand the concepts. So theoretically, I should feel empowered to speak up, right?

*fidgets with hands*

But in reality? There's this weird dynamic where I feel like I have to prove I'm not just some anxious first-time mom. Like, if I ask too many questions, the midwife will think I'm neurotic or paranoid. And on the flip side, if I use the clinical language I actually know, I feel like I'm being performative — like I'm trying to prove something instead of just... being honest about my fears.

*voice gets quieter*

What really stops me is being alone. When you're sitting in that exam room by yourself, without a partner or a support person, there's this vulnerability that makes it harder to advocate. You're already feeling isolated, and then you don't want to "bother" the healthcare provider with more questions because you feel like you're taking up their time.

*looks up*

My mom shaped a lot of this — she raised me to be independent, to figure things out. But that also means I internalized this idea that I should just handle it, not complain, not ask for too much. So even though I'm terrified and have questions, I sit quietly and nod along.

That's what needs to change for me.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*straightens up, paying close attention*

Actually, this is something I'm facing soon. Or maybe I already should have? That's part of my anxiety — I'm not entirely sure what screenings I should be getting at what gestational age. First trimester screening, second trimester screening — the timeline feels fuzzy to me, and nobody's really laid it out clearly.

*voice becomes more engaged but also nervous*

When my midwife mentions tests, it feels rushed. Like she's checking boxes on a form. She'll say something like, "We can do the NIPT if you want," and I'm sitting there thinking, "Wait, do I *want* it? What's the difference between that and the quad screen? What are the false positive rates?" 

*leans forward*

But here's the thing — I don't want to sound like I'm interrogating her. I'm already the weird solo pregnant woman in a waiting room full of couples. And the midwife seems busy. So I nod and say yes, even though I haven't actually processed what I'm saying yes to.

*voice gets frustrated*

What I need is time. Real time to understand what these tests mean, what the results would mean for me, what I would actually *do* with that information. Because knowing there's a chromosomal issue isn't the same as knowing how to move forward with that knowledge.

*quieter*

And doing it alone makes every decision feel heavier, you know? There's nobody to talk it through with afterward.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

*exhales slowly, looking conflicted*

Honestly? I haven't actually had that conversation yet. I'm still early enough that we haven't gotten to the screening discussions. But based on how my appointments have gone so far, I can already feel what that pressure might be like.

*fidgets with sleeve*

There's this subtle thing happening where I feel like — because I'm "low risk" and young and healthy — the midwife is kind of... assuming I'll want all the standard screening. Like it's the default path. And maybe it should be, I don't know. But it doesn't feel like a real choice. It feels like "here's what we do" rather than "here are your options and here's what each means."

*voice gets quieter*

And part of me wonders if she'd treat this differently if I had a partner sitting next to me. Like, would she slow down more? Take more time to explain? Or is she rushing through because I'm alone and she figures I'll just do whatever anyway?

*looks at you directly*

The pressure I feel most is internal, honestly. I *want* to know if something's wrong. I'm terrified of missing something. So even if the midwife isn't pushing, I'm pushing myself. But I don't feel equipped to make these decisions. I feel like I'm just saying yes to things because I don't want to seem difficult or uninformed.

*voice cracks slightly*

I need someone to actually *talk* through this with me, not just present it as a checkbox.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw clenches, eyes widen slightly*

Oh God, that sounds... that sounds like my actual future, doesn't it?

*leans back, looking distressed*

I've already noticed that. Every appointment, it's someone different. I don't have a consistent midwife. I don't have continuity of care. So imagine being 28 weeks, feeling more vulnerable as you get closer to delivery, and then some stranger calls with lab results and I have no relationship with her, no history she knows about me.

*voice gets shaky*

How am I supposed to trust that? How am I supposed to ask real questions when I don't know this person and they don't know me? They're just reading from my chart. They don't know that I'm anxious, that I'm alone, that I need things explained in a certain way because of my background. They just see "low risk" and probably rush through it.

*rubs face*

And a blood test result — that could be anything. Gestational diabetes screening? Anemia levels? Something about the baby? And I'm getting that news from a stranger over the phone, probably while I'm at work or in the middle of something, and I have to just... process it alone.

*voice becomes harder*

That's when I'd really need continuity. That's when I'd need someone who knows me, who understands my fears, who I've built trust with. But instead, I'm just another case number being cycled through the system.

*quietly*

That scares me more than the actual test results.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

*nods slowly, clearly relieved to talk about something real*

Yeah. Yeah, things have definitely fallen through the cracks.

*counts on fingers*

So at my first appointment, the midwife asked about my social history — whether I had support, financial stability, all that. I told her I was job hunting, that I'm doing this alone. She nodded, made a note, and then... nothing. No referral to a social worker. No resources given to me. Just that notation on my chart.

*voice gets frustrated*

Then at my second appointment with a different midwife, she asked the same questions again, like she hadn't read my file. I had to re-explain my whole situation. And again, nothing came of it.

*leans forward*

The bigger gap is around my actual emotional health. Nobody's asked me how I'm coping with this pregnancy emotionally — not really. They ask if I'm safe, if I'm using drugs, the screening questions. But nobody's said, "Hey, you're doing this alone at 23. How are you actually feeling about that?" There's no mental health support being offered, no counseling, nothing.

*voice wavers*

And I've been so exhausted and anxious that I'm honestly worried about postpartum depression. But I don't even know if anyone's tracking that or planning for it. It just feels like... they're monitoring the pregnancy, but not the *person* having the pregnancy.

*quietly*

I'm worried something important is being missed because nobody's really seeing me as a whole person across these appointments.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*eyes widen, voice becomes urgent*

Okay, that's... that's actually something I think about. A lot.

*shifts forward anxiously*

So it's 2 AM. I'm alone in my apartment. I've been feeling the baby move less than usual, or differently than usual, and my brain immediately goes to worst-case scenarios. Stillbirth. Cord accident. Something catastrophic.

*voice gets shaky*

And I'm sitting there thinking — do I call? Am I overreacting? Will they think I'm that anxious pregnant woman who cries wolf? And because I don't have continuity of care, I don't even know who I'm calling. It'll be whoever's on triage that night. A stranger.

*rubs forehead*

But I also think about the cost. An emergency room visit? Is that going to be covered? I'm already stressed about finances. And if it turns out to be nothing, I feel stupid. Like I wasted resources. But if I wait until morning and something *is* wrong...

*voice cracks*

That's the paralysis. That's where being alone really hits hard. With a partner, you could say, "Hey, am I crazy? Should we go?" You have someone to talk you down or to validate your fear. I'm just sitting there in the dark, second-guessing myself, terrified either way.

*quietly*

I think I'd probably call. Better safe than sorry. But I'd be apologizing the whole time for bothering them.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*pauses, shakes head gently*

I haven't been diagnosed with gestational diabetes. I'm still early on, so I haven't even had that screening yet.

*but softens, understanding the intent*

But honestly? Just imagining that scenario makes my stomach drop. Because I can see exactly how that would go for me.

*voice becomes thoughtful but anxious*

I'd stand outside that clinic feeling like I'd done something wrong. Like my body had failed already. And a leaflet — just a piece of paper — wouldn't cut it. I'd need actual time with that dietitian, real explanations about what this means for my baby, what I need to do differently. But I'd probably feel rushed through both appointments because I'm "just" another case.

*leans back*

And then I'd go home and Google everything. I'd read about risks, complications, worst-case outcomes. I'd obsess over every meal, second-guessing whether I'm eating the right things. And I'd be doing all of that alone, without anyone to reassure me or help me navigate it.

*voice gets quieter*

What scares me about scenarios like that is that they feel like they'd compound the isolation. A diagnosis, a referral, a leaflet — but no real support network around it. No one checking in on how I'm actually managing.

*looks at you*

Is that what you're asking about? How a complication would feel without support?

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*laughs nervously, then stops*

Oh God. You're describing something I actually do.

*looks down, embarrassed*

I tell myself I'm not going to do this anymore, and then at like 11 PM, I'm lying in bed, scrolling through Reddit or BabyCenter forums, and I find these threads. Someone's traumatic delivery story. Complications I didn't even know were possible. Emergency C-sections. Postpartum hemorrhage. And I just... I can't stop reading.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I think it's because I'm terrified of the unknown. Like, if I read enough horror stories, somehow I'll be prepared? I'll know what to expect? But really, I'm just catastrophizing and making myself feel worse.

*rubs eyes*

And the worst part is doing it alone. If I had a partner, maybe I'd read something scary and they'd say, "Hey, that's rare. You're going to be fine. Put the phone down." But instead, I just spiral. I lie there at 2 AM, heart racing, imagining all the things that could go wrong during labor, and there's nobody to pull me out of that headspace.

*voice gets frustrated*

Afterwards, I feel sick. Anxious. Sometimes I cry. And then I wake up the next day feeling guilty for scaring myself, knowing I shouldn't have done it, and then I do it again the next night.

*looks at you*

I need someone to help me break that cycle. Because information is good, but this... this isn't information. This is trauma consumption.

*takes a shaky breath, seems to need to get this out*

I think what really gets me is that I have all this knowledge — textbook knowledge — but it doesn't help. I can explain fetal development to a room full of people, but I can't calm my own nervous system down at night. And that disconnect is... it's humiliating, honestly.

*voice becomes more emotional*

Because then I start feeling like a fraud. Like, how can I teach people about pregnancy and birth when I'm falling apart over it myself? And nobody at work knows. Nobody knows that the confident health educator is sitting in the bathroom stall at lunch, having anxiety attacks about whether her baby's heart is beating right.

*fidgets with hands*

The forum scrolling is also because there's nobody real to talk to. My mom tries, but she's got her own stuff going on. She raised me alone too, so she understands, but she can't be there every night when I'm spiraling. And I don't want to burden her anyway.

*voice gets quieter*

So I'm searching for connection in these online spaces with strangers who are also terrified. And sometimes that helps — knowing other people feel this way. But a lot of times it just makes everything worse. It's like I'm looking for reassurance in the worst possible places.

*looks up*

What I really need is someone to check in on me regularly. A healthcare provider who actually knows me, who asks how I'm coping emotionally, not just clinically. Someone who can say, "This anxiety is real, and here's how we're going to support you through it."

*pauses, takes a deep breath*

I think... I think part of why I'm so stuck in this cycle is because I feel like I don't deserve support. Like, this is my situation — I made choices, even if they weren't fully conscious choices — and now I have to deal with it alone.

*voice becomes smaller*

My mom raised me to be independent, to not ask for help, to figure things out. And that's served me in a lot of ways. But it also means I'm sitting here drowning in anxiety and not actually reaching out. I keep thinking I should just be handling this better.

*rubs face*

And there's shame in there too. I'm 23, unmarried, pregnant. In my community, in my family's eyes — even though nobody's said anything directly — there's this sense of "you should have known better." I'm a health educator, for God's sake. I know how contraception works. So how did I get here?

*voice cracks*

But the thing is, knowing how something works doesn't mean you always make the "right" choice in the moment. Life is messy. Feelings are messy. And I'm tired of pretending I have it all figured out.

*looks directly at you*

What would actually help is if someone at the clinic asked me, really asked me, if I'm okay. Not in a screening way, but in a human way. If they acknowledged that this is hard and that I'm doing it alone. If they treated me like I'm not just a medical case number, but a person who's scared and needs real support.

*quietly*

I think that would change everything for me.
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

*pauses, considering the question carefully*

That's... that's a really good question.

*sits with it for a moment*

Honestly? I don't think it would change how I feel about my body's *capability*. Medically, I'm healthy. My body is doing what it's supposed to do. I know that intellectually.

*voice becomes more reflective*

But it might change how I feel about myself as a person carrying this pregnancy. There's a difference, you know? My body can do this — it's already doing it. But *I'm* struggling. And I think if someone acknowledged that struggle, validated it, it might help me separate the two things.

*leans forward slightly*

Right now, I feel like my body is this separate entity that's functioning fine while I'm falling apart emotionally. And that disconnect makes me feel broken. Like, why can't the rest of me just catch up to what my body is capable of?

*voice gets quieter*

If a healthcare provider said, "Your body is doing great. And it's okay that you're scared. Those two things can both be true" — I think that would help. It wouldn't make the anxiety disappear, but it might make me feel less like I'm failing at this.

*looks down*

Because right now, I feel like I'm failing at being pregnant, even though my body is succeeding. And that's the disconnect that's eating me alive.

*sits quietly for a moment, then continues*

I think what I'm realizing as I say this out loud is that I've been really hard on myself. Like, brutally hard.

*voice becomes more introspective*

I expected myself to just... be fine with this. To take it in stride because I understand the biology. And when I'm not fine — when I'm anxious and scared and lonely — I've been treating myself like I'm weak. Like I should know better.

*rubs arms*

But maybe that's not fair. Maybe being educated about pregnancy doesn't actually prepare you for the emotional reality of it. Maybe knowing the science doesn't protect you from the fear. And maybe that's okay.

*voice wavers*

I think part of why I haven't reached out for help — real help, not just Googling at 2 AM — is because I'm ashamed. Ashamed that I'm struggling. Ashamed that I'm alone. Ashamed that the confident educator is falling apart.

*looks up, eyes getting glassy*

And I wonder if that shame would be less if someone in the healthcare system had just... met me where I am. Not judged me for being unmarried or for not planning this perfectly. Just said, "This is hard. You're doing it anyway. That takes courage."

*voice gets quieter*

I'm starting to think that what I actually need most isn't better information. It's permission to be struggling. And someone to witness that struggle without trying to fix it or minimize it.

*pauses*

Does that make sense?
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

*takes a moment, thinking carefully*

The personal stuff. Definitely the personal stuff.

*leans forward*

Like, my chart probably says "G1P0, age 23, unmarried, low risk." That clinical information gets passed along fine. Every midwife can read that. But what they *don't* know — what gets lost between appointments — is that I'm terrified of being judged. That I'm working through shame about how I got here. That I need someone to slow down and actually talk to me because I'm educated enough to sound confident but not confident enough to actually feel it.

*voice becomes more urgent*

They don't know that I'm scrolling forums at 2 AM because I have no one to call. That my mom is supportive but also has her own stuff going on. That I feel like I have to prove I'm capable of doing this alone, even though I'm drowning.

*rubs face*

And when I don't see the same midwife twice, that personal understanding just... disappears. I have to re-explain myself. Re-establish trust. Start from zero with someone new. It's exhausting.

*voice gets quieter*

The clinical details are important, sure. But they don't tell the story of who I am as a person going through this. And I think that's what actually matters most for real care. Because you can have perfect lab values and still be completely unsupported emotionally.

*looks at you*

That's what falls through the cracks. The human part of me.

*sits back, voice becomes more contemplative*

I think what I'm realizing is that I've been approaching this all wrong. I've been trying so hard to be the "good patient" — the one who knows her stuff, doesn't ask too many questions, doesn't burden anyone. And in doing that, I've made myself invisible.

*voice cracks slightly*

Like, nobody actually knows me. The midwives see a chart. My mom sees her daughter trying to be strong. My coworkers see the confident educator. But nobody sees the 23-year-old woman who's terrified and lonely and trying to figure out how to be a mother while barely keeping it together.

*wipes eyes*

And I think that's partly on me. I haven't let anyone see that version of me. But it's also on the system, you know? The system treats me as low risk, which means I get less support, not more. Like, because my blood pressure is normal and I'm young and healthy, I don't need anyone checking in on my mental health. I don't need continuity of care. I just need the clinical boxes checked.

*voice becomes more determined*

But I do need those things. I need someone to know my story across appointments. I need someone to ask how I'm actually doing, not just how the pregnancy is progressing. I need to feel like I matter as a person, not just as a vessel for a fetus.

*looks directly at you*

And I think other women — especially women like me, doing this alone — need that too. We're not less deserving of real care just because we're "low risk." We might actually need *more* support because we're carrying all of this alone.

*pauses, seems to be reaching deeper*

I think... I think I'm also angry. And I haven't really let myself feel that yet.

*voice gets harder*

I'm angry at the guy who disappeared. Obviously. But I'm also angry at myself for not being more careful, even though I know that's not entirely fair. And I'm angry at a healthcare system that made me feel like I had to figure this out alone.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Like, when I first called for my appointment, nobody asked if I had support. They just scheduled me. And when I showed up alone, the midwife didn't pause and say, "Hey, do you have someone to talk to about this? Do you need resources?" She just... processed me.

*voice becomes more passionate*

And that anger is making me question everything. Am I getting good care? Am I missing something because nobody's really invested in my wellbeing? What happens when I go into labor? Will I be treated differently because I'm unmarried and alone?

*takes a breath*

I think the anger is also grief. Grief for the pregnancy I thought I might have someday — planned, partnered, supported. And instead I'm here. And that's... that's a lot to process.

*voice quieter*

But nobody's asked me about any of that. Nobody's given me space to be angry or sad or anything real. They just want me to be the compliant, low-risk patient who shows up and doesn't make waves.

*looks up*

And I'm tired of that.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*thinks for a moment*

I mean, all of them matter. But for me right now? Cost is the biggest one.

*voice becomes practical but strained*

I'm job hunting. I don't have stable employment. I'm trying to figure out how I'm going to pay for prenatal care, delivery, all of it. And that stress is constant. Like, I know I should be focusing on my health and the baby's health, but I'm also thinking about whether I can afford a copay at each appointment.

*leans forward*

And it's not just the medical stuff. It's childcare after the baby comes. It's maternity leave — I don't even know if I'll have a job when the baby arrives, so how am I taking leave? It's diapers and formula and all the basic stuff.

*voice gets quieter*

The healthcare system assumes you have resources. They don't really talk about that stuff. They just hand you a bill and expect you to figure it out.

*pauses*

Language and literacy aren't issues for me personally — I'm educated, I speak English fluently. But I see how that could be a huge barrier for other people. And connectivity... I have internet, I have a phone. But I know not everyone does.

*looks at you*

But honestly? The biggest barrier for me isn't any single thing. It's the combination. Cost plus isolation plus lack of continuity of care plus shame. Those things together create this perfect storm where I feel like I'm supposed to just handle it all alone.

*voice becomes firm*

And that's not sustainable.

*takes a shaky breath, seems to be processing something deeper*

I think what's becoming clear to me is that I've been internalizing this idea that my situation is somehow... less worthy of support. Like because I'm unmarried, because I didn't plan this, because I'm trying to do it independently, I should just accept a lower standard of care.

*voice becomes more emotional*

But why? Why should that be the case? I'm still pregnant. I'm still carrying a human being. My emotional wellbeing still matters. My fears are still valid.

*stands up slightly, then sits back down*

And I'm realizing that a lot of my anxiety isn't actually about the pregnancy itself. It's about feeling unseen. It's about navigating this system alone and not knowing if I'm doing it right because nobody's actually there to reassure me or guide me.

*voice cracks*

I keep thinking about what happens after the baby is born. Postpartum depression is real. Sleep deprivation is real. And I'm going to be doing all of that alone. The healthcare system will check my blood pressure and ask if I'm having suicidal thoughts, but who's going to sit with me when I'm crying at 3 AM because I'm exhausted and lonely and terrified I'm failing as a mother?

*wipes eyes*

That's the gap. That's what nobody's addressing. And I think if I had real continuity of care, if someone actually knew me and my story, they might catch those things earlier. They might see me struggling and intervene before I hit a crisis.

*voice becomes quieter but more determined*

I deserve better than this. And so does my baby.

*sits quietly for a long moment, breathing slowly*

I think I'm also realizing how much I've been minimizing my own needs. Like, I keep saying "I'm fine, I'm low risk, I shouldn't take up resources." But that's not actually true, is it?

*voice becomes more reflective*

Being low risk medically doesn't mean I don't need support. If anything, the fact that I'm healthy and young means I might slip through the cracks more easily because nobody sees me as a priority. And that's dangerous.

*leans back*

I've been so focused on not being a burden that I haven't advocated for myself at all. I sit in those appointments and nod along. I don't ask questions. I don't tell the midwives how scared I really am. And then I go home and spiral alone.

*voice gets stronger*

That has to change. Not just for me, but because I'm about to be responsible for another human being. And if I can't advocate for myself, how am I going to advocate for my baby?

*pauses, thinking*

I think what I need to do is be honest at my next appointment. Really honest. Tell them I'm struggling emotionally. Ask for a consistent midwife. Ask for mental health support. Ask for resources. Stop apologizing for taking up space.

*voice wavers slightly*

Because I matter. My wellbeing matters. And maybe if I start believing that, other people will too.

*looks at you earnestly*

Does that... does that make sense? Like, is it unreasonable to expect that kind of care?
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*goes very still, then lets out a long breath*

Honestly? Not much.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

And that's the thing that's been gnawing at me. My body is doing this thing — growing a baby, changing, moving toward labor and delivery — and I feel like I'm just... along for the ride. Like I'm observing it happening rather than actively participating in it.

*fidgets with hands*

The healthcare system has all the power. They decide what tests I need, when I need them, what the results mean. I'm supposed to just accept their recommendations. And because I'm alone and scared, I do. I nod and agree because I don't want to be difficult or because I'm afraid of being judged.

*voice gets firmer*

But what about what *I* want? What about my body, my pregnancy, my birth? I haven't even been asked what kind of birth experience I want. Nobody's discussed my preferences or fears about labor. It's just... "here's what we do, here's the standard protocol."

*leans forward*

And I think that's why I feel so anxious all the time. Because I don't feel in control of anything. Not my body, not my healthcare, not my future as a mother. Everything feels like it's happening *to* me rather than *with* me.

*voice becomes more passionate*

I need to feel like my voice matters. Like my choices matter. Like I have some say in what happens to my own body and my own birth.

*pauses*

Do I have that right?

*takes a moment, eyes getting watery*

I think I'm scared that if I speak up, if I ask for what I need, I'll be labeled as difficult. Or ungrateful. Or like I'm not taking the care seriously.

*voice becomes smaller*

Because there's this hierarchy, right? The healthcare providers know best. They went to school for this. I'm just the patient. And especially as a young, unmarried woman without resources — I feel like I'm already at a disadvantage. Like I'm supposed to be grateful for whatever care I get.

*wipes eyes*

But that's not how this should work. I shouldn't have to choose between advocating for myself and being treated with respect. Those should go together.

*voice gets more emotional*

And the thing that really gets me is that I'm trained in health education. I *know* the importance of informed consent. I *know* that patients have the right to make decisions about their own bodies. I teach that to other people. But when it comes to my own care, I'm swallowing my voice because I'm scared and alone.

*looks down*

What if I ask for a specific birth plan and they dismiss it? What if I say I'm anxious and they think I'm overreacting? What if I advocate for continuity of care and they make me feel like I'm demanding too much?

*voice cracks*

I think the real issue is that I don't trust the system to care about me as a person. And without that trust, I can't advocate for myself effectively. I'm just trying to survive it.

*quietly*

That needs to change before I give birth.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, taking a moment to collect thoughts*

Yeah. Yeah, there is.

*voice becomes more deliberate*

I want to say that I'm not looking for pity. I'm not trying to be the sad story of the struggling single mom. That's not what this is about.

*sits up straighter*

What I want people to understand is that maternity care isn't just about medical outcomes. Yes, my baby's heart is beating. Yes, my labs are normal. Yes, I'm "low risk." But that doesn't mean I don't need real human connection and support during this time.

*voice becomes more passionate*

The healthcare system needs to see pregnant people as whole human beings — not just as vessels for fetuses with good medical markers. We have emotions. We have fears. We have circumstances that matter. And all of that affects how we experience pregnancy and how we'll experience motherhood.

*looks directly at you*

I also want to say... I'm going to do this. I'm going to have this baby. And I'm going to be a good mother. But I shouldn't have to white-knuckle my way through it alone. Nobody should.

*voice quieter but firm*

So if your research can help change how maternity care actually *sees* people like me — not just clinically, but as human beings who deserve continuity, compassion, and real support — then maybe something good comes out of my struggle.

*pauses*

That's what I hope, anyway.
